subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
solution:
Recall the formula of de-Broglie wavelength, λ=hp, here p is the linear momentum of the particle, now use the relation between linear momentum and kinetic energy, p=2Em⇒E∝1λ2⇒E=kλ2

and E'=16k9λ2⇒ΔE=7E9.

subject: Physics
q:
The temperature of an ideal gas in three dimensions is 300 K. The corresponding de-Broglie wavelength of the electron approximately at 300 K is:

me=mass of electron =9×10-31 kg, h=Planck constant =6.6×10-34 J s, kB=Boltzmann constant=1.38×10-23 J K-1
question:
The temperature of an ideal gas in three dimensions is 300 K. The corresponding de-Broglie wavelength of the electron approximately at 300 K is:

me=mass of electron =9×10-31 kg, h=Planck constant =6.6×10-34 J s, kB=Boltzmann constant=1.38×10-23 J K-1
solution:
k=1.38×10-23 J K-1

Mass of electron =9×10-31 kg

⇒λD=h2m×32×kT=h3mkT

⇒λD=6.6×10-343×9×10-31×1.38×10-23×300=6.6×10-34105.72×10-27=62 Aο

Thus x=62 Aο

subject: Physics
q: Photoelectrons are ejected from a metal when light of frequency $v$ falls on it. Pick out the wrong statement from the following.
question: Photoelectrons are ejected from a metal when light of frequency $v$ falls on it. Pick out the wrong statement from the following.
options: No electrons are emitted if $v$ is less than $W / h$, where $W$ is the work function of the metal
The ejection of the photoelectrons is instantaneous.
The maximum energy of the photoelectrons is hu.
The maximum energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of the light.
solution: According to photo-electric equation : $\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} v-\mathrm{h} v_0$ (Work function) Some sort of energy is used in ejecting the photoelectrons.

subject: Physics
q: A proton moving with one tenth of velocity of light has a certain de Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. An alpha particle having certain kinetic energy has the same de-Brogle wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The ratio of kinetic energy of proton and that of alpha particle is :
question: A proton moving with one tenth of velocity of light has a certain de Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. An alpha particle having certain kinetic energy has the same de-Brogle wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The ratio of kinetic energy of proton and that of alpha particle is :
solution:
As we know, de Broglie wavelength is given by,

λ=hp

Now kinetic energy can be written as,

K=12mv2=p22m=h22mλ2

Therefore,

K∝1m

Hence, required ratio

KpKα=mαmp=4⇒mα:mp=4:1

subject: Physics
q: UV light of $4.13 \mathrm{eV}$ is incident on a photosensitive metal surface having work function $3.13 \mathrm{eV}$. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectrons will be:
question: UV light of $4.13 \mathrm{eV}$ is incident on a photosensitive metal surface having work function $3.13 \mathrm{eV}$. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectrons will be:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_{\text {photon }}=(\text { work function })+\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\max } \\ & \therefore 4.13=3.13+\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\max } \\ & \therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{max}}=1 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$

question: According to EInstein's photoelectric equation, the plot of the kinetic energy of the emitted photo electrons from a metal $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}$ the frequency, of the incident radiation gives straight line whose slope

depends on the intensity of the radiation

is the same for all metals and independent of the intensity of the radiation.

subject: Physics
q:
A beam of electrons of energy E scatters from a target having atomic spacing of 1 Å. The first maximum intensity occurs at θ=60°. Then E(in eV) is........
(Planck's constant h=6.64×10-34 Js, 1 eV=1.6×10-19J, electron mass m=9.1×10-31 kg)
question:
A beam of electrons of energy E scatters from a target having atomic spacing of 1 Å. The first maximum intensity occurs at θ=60°. Then E(in eV) is........
(Planck's constant h=6.64×10-34 Js, 1 eV=1.6×10-19J, electron mass m=9.1×10-31 kg)
solution:
2dsinθ=nλ

2d32=1λ

d=1 Å

λ=3 Å

λ=3=150V

V=50 volt

E=50 eV

subject: Physics
q: When photon of energy <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> strikes the surface of a metal <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the ejected photoelectrons have maximum kinetic energy <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and de-Broglie wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons liberated from another metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> by photon of energy <math><mn>4.50</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>1.5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the de-Broglie wavelength of these photoelectrons <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> then the work function of metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
question: When photon of energy <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> strikes the surface of a metal <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the ejected photoelectrons have maximum kinetic energy <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and de-Broglie wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons liberated from another metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> by photon of energy <math><mn>4.50</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>1.5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the de-Broglie wavelength of these photoelectrons <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> then the work function of metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
solution:
Relation between de-Broglie wavelength and K.E. is
λ=h2(KE)me⇒λ∝1KE
λAλB=KEBKEA
⇒12=TA-1.5TA⇒TA=2 eV
∴KEB=2-1.5=0.5 eV
ϕB=4.5-0.5=4 eV

subject: Physics
q:
The surface of certain metal is first illuminated with light of wavelength λ1=350 nm and then, by a light of wavelength λ2=540 nm. It is found that the maximum speed of the photoelectrons in the two cases differ by a factor of 2. The work function of the metal (in eV) is close to

(Energy of photon =1240λin nm eV )
question:
The surface of certain metal is first illuminated with light of wavelength λ1=350 nm and then, by a light of wavelength λ2=540 nm. It is found that the maximum speed of the photoelectrons in the two cases differ by a factor of 2. The work function of the metal (in eV) is close to

(Energy of photon =1240λin nm eV )
solution:
λ1=350 nm,  λ2=540 nm,  V1V2=2⇒V1=2V and V2=V

hcλ1=ϕ+12m2V2

hcλ2=ϕ+12mV2

hcλ1-4hcλ2=ϕ-4ϕ

hc4540-1340=3ϕ

ϕ=1.8 eV

subject: Physics
q: When the wavelength of radiation falling on a metal is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mi>nm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>to</mi><mo> </mo><mn>200</mn><mi>nm</mi></math>, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons becomes three times larger. The work function of the metal is close to:
question: When the wavelength of radiation falling on a metal is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mi>nm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>to</mi><mo> </mo><mn>200</mn><mi>nm</mi></math>, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons becomes three times larger. The work function of the metal is close to:
solution:
KEmax=hcλ-ϕ=hc500-ϕ   .....(i)

Now,

3KEmax=hc200-ϕ

   .....(ii)

From equation (i) and (ii)

iii=31=hc200-ϕhc500-ϕ

Put the value of hc=1237.5 and solving ϕ=0.61eV

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic neon lamp with wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>670</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> illuminates a photo-sensitive material which has a stopping voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. What will be the stopping voltage if the source light is changed with another source of wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>474</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> ?
question: A monochromatic neon lamp with wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>670</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> illuminates a photo-sensitive material which has a stopping voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. What will be the stopping voltage if the source light is changed with another source of wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>474</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> ?
solution:
eVs=hcλ-ϕ

eV'-V=hc1λ'-1λ

V'=hce1λ'-1λ+V

=12401474-1670+0.48=1.25eV

subject: Physics
q:
In photoelectric effect, the stopping potential $\left(\mathrm{V}_0\right) \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ frequency $(\nu)$ curve is plotted.
( h is the Planck's constant and $\phi_0$ is work function of metal )
(A) $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ is linear.
(B) The slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve $=\frac{\phi_0}{\mathrm{~h}}$
(C) h constant is related to the slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ line.
(D) The value of electric charge of electron is not required to determine $h$ using the $V_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve.
(E) The work function can be estimated without knowing the value of $h$.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
In photoelectric effect, the stopping potential $\left(\mathrm{V}_0\right) \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ frequency $(\nu)$ curve is plotted.
( h is the Planck's constant and $\phi_0$ is work function of metal )
(A) $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ is linear.
(B) The slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve $=\frac{\phi_0}{\mathrm{~h}}$
(C) h constant is related to the slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ line.
(D) The value of electric charge of electron is not required to determine $h$ using the $V_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve.
(E) The work function can be estimated without knowing the value of $h$.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{hv}=\phi+\mathrm{KE}_{\max } \\
& \mathrm{KE}_{\max }=\mathrm{eV}_0 \\
& \mathrm{~V}_0=\frac{\mathrm{hv}-\phi}{\mathrm{e}}
\end{aligned}$
(A) $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \mathrm{V}$ is linear correct
(B) Slope
$\mathrm{v}_0=\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}\right) \mathrm{v}-\frac{\phi}{\mathrm{e}} \text { Wrong }$

Slope $\frac{h}{e}$
(C) Correct
(D) Incorrect
(E) Correct

subject: Physics
q: If the total energy transferred to a surface in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>, then the magnitude of the total momentum delivered to this surface for complete absorption will be :
question: If the total energy transferred to a surface in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>, then the magnitude of the total momentum delivered to this surface for complete absorption will be :
solution:
As we know,

p=Ec, where c is velocity of light.

Therefore, p=6.48×1053×108=2.16×10-3 kg m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
A metal exposed to light of wavelength 800 nm and emits photoelectrons with a certain kinetic energy. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electron doubles when light of wavelength 500 nm is used. The work function of the metal is

(Take hc=1230 eV-nm).
question:
A metal exposed to light of wavelength 800 nm and emits photoelectrons with a certain kinetic energy. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electron doubles when light of wavelength 500 nm is used. The work function of the metal is

(Take hc=1230 eV-nm).
solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation

kmax=hcλ-ϕ

⇒k1=1230800-ϕ       ...1

And ⇒k2=1230500-ϕ

Since, k2=2k1

⇒2k1=1230500-ϕ    ...2

Eliminating k1 from (1) and (2) we get

0=1230500-1230400+ϕ

⇒ϕ=0.615 eV

q: A particle <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> is formed due to a completely inelastic collision of particles <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> having de-Broglie wavelengths <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> respectively. If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> were moving in opposite directions, then the de-Broglie wavelength of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> is:
question: A particle <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> is formed due to a completely inelastic collision of particles <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> having de-Broglie wavelengths <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> respectively. If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> were moving in opposite directions, then the de-Broglie wavelength of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></math> is:
solution:
hλ=hλx-hλy
1λ=1λx-1λy
λ=λxλyλx-λy

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement $-1$ and Statement $-2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement-1 : A metallic surface is irradiated by a monochromatic light of frequency $v>v_0$ (the threshold frequency). The maximum kinetic energy and the stopping potential are $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{V}_0$ respectively. If the frequency incident on the surface doubled, both the $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{V}_0$ are also doubled.
Statement-2 : The maximum kinetic energy and the stopping potential of photoelectrons emitted from a surface are linearly dependent on the frequency of incident light.
question:
This question has Statement $-1$ and Statement $-2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement-1 : A metallic surface is irradiated by a monochromatic light of frequency $v>v_0$ (the threshold frequency). The maximum kinetic energy and the stopping potential are $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{V}_0$ respectively. If the frequency incident on the surface doubled, both the $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{V}_0$ are also doubled.
Statement-2 : The maximum kinetic energy and the stopping potential of photoelectrons emitted from a surface are linearly dependent on the frequency of incident light.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.

subject: Physics
q:
The stopping potential Vo (in volt) as a function of frequency (v) for a sodium emitter, is shown in the figure. The work function of sodium, form the data plotted in the figure, will be:
(Given: Planck’s constant h=6.63×10-34 J s , electron charge ( e ) =1.6×10-19 C)
question:
The stopping potential Vo (in volt) as a function of frequency (v) for a sodium emitter, is shown in the figure. The work function of sodium, form the data plotted in the figure, will be:
(Given: Planck’s constant h=6.63×10-34 J s , electron charge ( e ) =1.6×10-19 C)
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, hν=ϕ+eV0  .

⇒V0=hνe-ϕe  ......1

At ν=4×1014 Hz, V0=0.

Putting the value in equation 1, we get

 0=hve-ϕe

So, Work function (ϕ)=hνe  in eV
=6.63×10-34×4×10141.6×10-19
=1.66 eV

subject: Physics
q: An oil drop of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with a density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is held stationary under a constant electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the Millikan's oil drop experiment. What is the number of excess electrons that the oil drop will possess? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>consider</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
question: An oil drop of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with a density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is held stationary under a constant electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the Millikan's oil drop experiment. What is the number of excess electrons that the oil drop will possess? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>consider</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
solution:
qE=Mg⇒neE=ρ43πr3×g
⇒n×1.6×10-19×3.55×105=3×103×43×π×2×10-33×9.81
⇒n=173×10(3-9-5+19)⇒n=1.73×1010.

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> associated with the electron orbiting in the second excited state of hydrogen atom is related to that in the ground state <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>G</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by:
question: The de-Broglie wavelength <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> associated with the electron orbiting in the second excited state of hydrogen atom is related to that in the ground state <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>G</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by:
solution:
We know that wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum, let λB is de-Broglie Wavelength corresponds to momentum PB, similarly  let λG is Wavelength at ground state corresponds to momentumPG, 
λBλG= PGPB= mvGmvB, and velocity, V∝zn, where Z is the atomic number, and n is a number of orbits, here, nG=1 (ground state), nB=3 (second excited state). So, λBλG= nBnG= 31⇒λB=3λG

subject: Physics
q: The anode voltage of a photocell is kept fixed. The wavelength <math> <mrow> <mi>λ</mi> </mrow> </math> of the light falling on the cathode is gradually changed. The plate current <math> <mrow> <mtext>I</mtext> </mrow> </math> of the photocell varies as follows :
question: The anode voltage of a photocell is kept fixed. The wavelength <math> <mrow> <mi>λ</mi> </mrow> </math> of the light falling on the cathode is gradually changed. The plate current <math> <mrow> <mtext>I</mtext> </mrow> </math> of the photocell varies as follows :
solution: As λ is increased, there will be a value of λ above which photo-electron will be cease to come out, so photo-current will becomes zero.

subject: Physics
q: An electron (mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>) with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is moving in an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is constant. If at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, de-Broglie wavelength is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math>, then its de-Broglie wavelength after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given by
question: An electron (mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>) with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is moving in an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is constant. If at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, de-Broglie wavelength is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math>, then its de-Broglie wavelength after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given by
solution:
Initial de-Broglie wavelength of electron,

λ0=hmv0 ....(i)

Force on electron in electric field,

F=-eE=-e-E0i^=eE0i^

Acceleration of electron, a=Fm=eE0i^m
Velocity of electron after time t,

v=v0i^+eE0i^mt=v0+eE0mti^=v01+eE0mv0ti^

de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron at time t is

λ=hmv

⇒λ=hmv01+eE0mv0t=λ01+eE0mv0t

subject: Physics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the maximum kinetic energies of photo-electrons emitted when two monochromatic beams of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively are incident on a metallic surface. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> then :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the maximum kinetic energies of photo-electrons emitted when two monochromatic beams of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively are incident on a metallic surface. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> then :
solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation

h⁡cλ=ϕ0+KEmax, where ϕ0 is a work function of a metal

∴ K⁡ 1 = h⁡ c λ 1 - ϕ 0 … i

        K⁡ 2 = h⁡ c λ 2 - ϕ 0 … i i

orK1-K2=hc1λ1-1λ2

                      =hc13λ2-1λ2=-2hc3λ2Givenλ1=3λ2

                      = - 2 3 K⁡ 2 + ϕ 0 Using (ii)

or⁡ K⁡ 1 = K⁡ 2 - 2 3 K⁡ 2 - 2 3 ϕ 0 = K⁡ 2 3 - 2 3 ϕ 0

or K⁡ 1 < K⁡ 2 3

subject: Physics
q:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements
Statement 1 : Davisson - germer experiment established the wave nature of electrons.
Statement 2 : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.
question:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements
Statement 1 : Davisson - germer experiment established the wave nature of electrons.
Statement 2 : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.
options: Statement $1$ is false, Statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is false
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is the correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is true, Statement $2$ is not the correct explanation for statement $1$.
solution: Davisson - Germer experiment showed that electron beams can undergo diffraction when passed through atomic crystals. This shows the wave nature of electrons as waves can exhibit interference and diffraction.

subject: Physics
q: $K_1$ and $K_2$ be the maximum kinetic energies of photoelectrons emitted from a surface of a given material for the light of wavelength $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, respectively. If $\lambda_1=2\lambda_2$ then the work function of material is given by:
question: $K_1$ and $K_2$ be the maximum kinetic energies of photoelectrons emitted from a surface of a given material for the light of wavelength $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, respectively. If $\lambda_1=2\lambda_2$ then the work function of material is given by:
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_1} - \phi$

$K_2 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} - \phi$

Given $\lambda_1 = 2\lambda_2$, the first equation becomes:

$K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda_2} - \phi$

Multiplying by $2$:

$2K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} - 2\phi$

Substituting $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} = K_2 + \phi$ from the second equation:

$2K_1 = (K_2 + \phi) - 2\phi$

$2K_1 = K_2 - \phi$

$\phi = K_2 - 2K_1$

Answer: $K_2-2K_1$

q: Given figure shows few data points in a photo-electric effect experiment for a certain metal. The minimum energy for ejection of electrons from its surface is: (Planck's constant h=6.62×10–34 J.s)
question: Given figure shows few data points in a photo-electric effect experiment for a certain metal. The minimum energy for ejection of electrons from its surface is: (Planck's constant h=6.62×10–34 J.s)
solution:
Threshold Energy =hv

=6.62×10−34×5.5×1014 J

Work friction =6.62×5.5×10−201.6×10−19eV

=2.27 eV

subject: Physics
q: The work function of a metal is 3 eV. The color of the visible light that is required to cause emission of photoelectrons is
question: The work function of a metal is 3 eV. The color of the visible light that is required to cause emission of photoelectrons is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& (\mathrm{KE})_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi \\ & \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda} \gt \phi[\text { for emission }] \\ & \lambda \lt \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\phi} \Rightarrow \lambda \lt \frac{1242}{3} \mathrm{~nm}
\end{aligned}$
So blue light option (B)

subject: Physics
q: Proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will have same de-Broglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1849</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math>)
question: Proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will have same de-Broglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1849</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math>)
solution:
The De Broglie wavelength is λ=hmv

The momentum is given by p=mv

The momentum of proton is pp=mpvp

The momentum of electron is  p'=meve

Since h is the Planck's constant and it is given that both wavelengths are same, i.e.

λp=λe, so pp=p'.

subject: Physics
q: In a photoelectric experiment, increasing the intensity of incident light:
question: In a photoelectric experiment, increasing the intensity of incident light:
options: increases the number of photons incident and also increases the K.E. of the ejected electrons.
increases the frequency of photons incident and increases the K.E. of the ejected electrons.
increases the number of photons incident and the K.E. of the ejected electrons remains unchanged.
increases the frequency of photons incident and the K.E. of the ejected electrons remains unchanged.
solution: The relation between the energy of photons and the intensity of photons is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>I</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mi>n</mi></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is the number of photons, if we increase the number of photons then frequency does not change it means total energy and kinetic energy of photon remains unchanged but due to to this property the intensity of light is increased.

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: A metallic surface is irradiated by a monochromatic light of frequency $v>v_0$ (the threshold frequency). If the incident frequency is now doubled, the photocurrent and the maximum kinetic energy are also doubled.
Statement 2: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted from a surface is linearly dependent on the frequency of the incident light. The photocurrent depends only on the intensity of the incident light.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: A metallic surface is irradiated by a monochromatic light of frequency $v>v_0$ (the threshold frequency). If the incident frequency is now doubled, the photocurrent and the maximum kinetic energy are also doubled.
Statement 2: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted from a surface is linearly dependent on the frequency of the incident light. The photocurrent depends only on the intensity of the incident light.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depends upon frequency on incident light and photo current depends upon intensity of incident light.
